posts.title: First-Time Pool Owner? Everything You Need to Know in North DFW
posts.excerpt: Just got a pool? Here's the no-fluff guide to equipment, water chemistry, weekly maintenance, and the 5 expensive mistakes every new Texas pool owner should avoid.
posts.content:
You just moved into a house with a pool. Maybe you built one. Either way, you're standing in your backyard looking at thousands of gallons of water and a bunch of equipment you've never touched before. Welcome to pool ownership in Texas.

The good news is that pool maintenance isn't rocket science. The bad news is that most first-time owners learn by making expensive mistakes — green water, burned-out pumps, cracked pipes from a freeze they didn't prepare for. In North DFW, where pools run nearly year-round and weather swings from 100°F summers to occasional hard freezes, the learning curve is steeper than in most parts of the country.

This guide covers everything you actually need to know — no fluff, no upselling, just the practical knowledge that keeps your pool clean, your equipment running, and your costs predictable.

## Know Your Equipment

Before you touch any chemicals, walk out to your equipment pad and identify what you're working with. Every pool in North DFW has these core components:

**Pool pump** — the motor that circulates water through the system. It pulls water from the skimmers and main drain, pushes it through the filter, and returns it to the pool. This is the most critical piece of equipment you own. If the pump stops, everything stops.

**Filter** — traps dirt, debris, and microscopic particles. There are three types: cartridge (most common in newer Frisco and Prosper homes), sand, and DE (diatomaceous earth). Each cleans differently and has different maintenance requirements.

**Skimmers** — the rectangular openings on the pool wall at water level. They catch floating debris before it sinks. Inside each one is a basket that needs to be emptied regularly.

**Return jets** — the fittings on the pool wall that push filtered water back into the pool. If these feel weak, it usually means your filter is dirty or your pump is struggling.

**Heater (if you have one)** — gas or electric, used to extend your swim season. Not all North DFW pools have them, but they're increasingly common in Plano and McKinney.

**Salt cell (if saltwater)** — the device that converts salt into chlorine. If your pool is saltwater, this is an additional component that needs periodic cleaning and eventual replacement.

**Automation panel (if equipped)** — controls pump schedules, lighting, heater, and other features from a panel or phone app. Common in newer builds.

Take a photo of every piece of equipment and its model number. You'll need this information if anything breaks.

## Understand Your Water Chemistry

Pool chemistry sounds intimidating, but there are really only five numbers you need to track:

**Free chlorine: 1 to 3 ppm** — this is your sanitizer. It kills bacteria, algae, and pathogens. Too low and your pool turns green. Too high and it irritates skin and eyes.

**pH: 7.2 to 7.6** — measures how acidic or basic your water is. Below 7.2, water corrodes metal equipment. Above 7.6, chlorine becomes ineffective and calcium starts scaling your tiles.

**Total alkalinity: 80 to 120 ppm** — acts as a buffer that prevents pH from swinging wildly. Get this right and pH becomes much easier to manage.

**Calcium hardness: 200 to 400 ppm** — North DFW tap water is already moderately hard, so this often runs high. Too much calcium causes white scale deposits on tiles and equipment.

**Cyanuric acid (stabilizer): 30 to 50 ppm** — protects chlorine from being destroyed by UV sunlight. In Texas, this is essential. Without it, you'll burn through chlorine in hours.

Buy a reliable test kit — liquid reagent kits (like Taylor K-2006) are more accurate than strips. Test your water at least twice a week during summer and once a week in winter.

## Set Your Pump Schedule

Your pump needs to run long enough each day to turn over the entire volume of your pool at least once. For most residential pools in North DFW (12,000 to 20,000 gallons), that means:

**Summer (April through October):** 8 to 12 hours per day. Run it during the hottest part of the day when chlorine demand is highest.

**Winter (November through March):** 4 to 6 hours per day. Enough to maintain circulation and prevent stagnation.

**During freezes:** Run the pump continuously. Moving water doesn't freeze. This single habit prevents the most expensive damage Texas pool owners face — cracked pipes and blown pump housings.

If you have a variable speed pump, run it on low speed for longer hours rather than high speed for fewer hours. You'll get better filtration and significantly lower electricity costs.

## The Weekly Maintenance Routine

Consistent weekly maintenance prevents 90% of pool problems. Here's what to do every week:

**Skim the surface** — remove leaves, bugs, and debris with a leaf net. In spring (pollen season) and fall (leaf drop), you may need to do this daily.

**Brush the walls and floor** — algae starts as an invisible film on surfaces before it becomes visible in the water. Brushing once a week disrupts it before it takes hold. Pay attention to steps, corners, and behind ladders where circulation is weakest.

**Clean the skimmer baskets** — pull the baskets out and dump them. A full basket restricts water flow to the pump and can cause it to lose prime.

**Check the pump basket** — turn off the pump, open the pump lid, and clean out the strainer basket. This catches debris that gets past the skimmers.

**Test and adjust chemicals** — test chlorine, pH, and alkalinity. Adjust as needed. In Texas summers, you'll likely need to add chlorine and pH adjuster (muriatic acid) every week.

**Check filter pressure** — your filter has a pressure gauge. Note the "clean" pressure after a fresh cleaning. When pressure rises 8 to 10 psi above that baseline, it's time to clean or backwash the filter.

This entire routine takes about 30 to 45 minutes once you get the hang of it.

## Monthly and Quarterly Tasks

**Monthly:** Deep clean or backwash your filter. Test calcium hardness and cyanuric acid. Inspect equipment for leaks, unusual noises, or corrosion.

**Quarterly:** Have your water professionally tested at a pool store — they'll check for metals, phosphates, and other parameters your home kit doesn't measure. Inspect all O-rings and gaskets for wear.

**Annually:** Have a professional inspect your equipment thoroughly — pump seals, heater connections, electrical components, and plumbing. This catches problems before they become emergencies.

## The Five Most Expensive Mistakes First-Time Owners Make

### 1. Ignoring the pump during a freeze
One hard freeze with the pump off can crack your pipes, pump housing, and filter — $1,500 to $3,000+ in damage. Run the pump continuously when temperatures drop below 32°F. Set a phone alarm if you have to.

### 2. Letting the pool go green
Once algae takes over, cleanup costs $300 to $1,200 depending on severity. It also clogs your filter and strains your pump. Preventing algae (maintaining chlorine levels and brushing weekly) costs almost nothing.

### 3. Running the pump too few hours
Short pump run times cause poor circulation, dead zones, and chemical imbalance. This leads to cloudy water and algae. It feels like you're saving on electricity, but you're creating expensive problems.

### 4. Never cleaning the filter
A dirty filter can reduce circulation by 30 to 40%. The pump works harder to compensate, shortening its lifespan. Filter cleaning costs very little — pump replacement costs $800 to $2,000.

### 5. Over-relying on chemicals to fix mechanical problems
If your water is cloudy but your chemical levels are fine, the problem is almost certainly filtration or circulation — not chemistry. Adding more chlorine to a pool with a dirty filter is like putting air freshener in a car with a dead skunk in the trunk.

## Texas-Specific Things Nobody Tells You

**Evaporation is real.** Your pool can lose a quarter inch of water per day in peak summer. That's normal. If you're losing more than that, you might have a leak. Do the bucket test — fill a bucket to the same level as your pool and set it on the first step. If the pool drops more than the bucket over 24 hours, call a leak detection pro.

**Pollen season will test your patience.** March through May in North DFW is brutal for pools. Oak and pecan pollen creates a yellow film on the surface, clogs filters fast, and introduces nitrogen that feeds algae while neutralizing chlorine. Skim daily and clean filters more frequently during pollen season.

**CYA buildup is a silent problem.** Chlorine tablets (trichlor) add cyanuric acid every time you use them. Over time, CYA builds up and makes your chlorine less effective. If CYA exceeds 80 ppm, the only fix is draining and refilling part of your pool. Test it quarterly.

**Your water bill will go up.** Between evaporation, backwashing, and splash-out, plan on your water bill increasing $30 to $60 per month during swim season.

**Insurance matters.** Make sure your homeowner's insurance covers your pool and any liability associated with it. An umbrella policy is worth considering.

## When to Call a Professional

Handle the weekly basics yourself if you want — it's manageable once you build the habit. But call a professional when:

- Your pool turns green and you can't see the bottom
- Equipment makes unusual noises or stops working
- You notice a significant water loss
- Pressure gauge reads abnormally high or low
- You see cracks, leaks, or water around equipment
- Chemical levels won't balance despite repeated adjustments
- Before and after freeze events if you're not comfortable managing it yourself

Many homeowners start with DIY maintenance and eventually switch to professional weekly service because the time savings and peace of mind are worth more than the cost. There's no wrong answer — what matters is that the pool gets consistent attention.

posts.title: Pool Service in Allen, TX: What Homeowners Need to Know
posts.excerpt: Allen pools face heavy pollen, aging equipment, and hard water. Here's what local homeowners need to know about maintaining a pool in Allen, TX.
posts.content:
Allen, TX sits right in the heart of Collin County — a fast-growing city with established neighborhoods, newer developments, and a lot of backyard pools. Most homes in communities like Twin Creeks, The Villages of Allen, Waterford Parks, and Montgomery Farm have pools that need year-round attention.

What makes Allen different from neighboring cities like Frisco or McKinney is the mix of pool ages. Older Allen neighborhoods have pools that are 15 to 20+ years old with aging equipment. Newer developments near Highway 75 and Bethany Drive have modern builds with automation and variable speed pumps. Each type needs different care, and a cookie-cutter approach doesn't cut it.

This guide covers what Allen homeowners specifically deal with — from common equipment issues to seasonal challenges — and what to look for in a pool service that actually understands this market.

## Why Allen Pools Need Consistent Maintenance

Allen's climate is identical to the rest of North DFW — long hot summers, unpredictable spring weather, occasional winter freezes, and heavy pollen from March through May. But Allen has a few local factors that affect pools specifically.

**Mature trees throughout the city.** Older Allen neighborhoods like Ridgeview and Breckenridge have well-established landscaping. Large oaks, pecans, and crape myrtles drop leaves, pollen, and small branches into pools year-round. Fall is particularly demanding — leaf load overwhelms skimmers and filters if they're not cleaned frequently.

**Aging infrastructure in older pools.** Many Allen pools were built in the early 2000s during the city's rapid growth phase. That means equipment is hitting the 15 to 20 year mark where pumps, heaters, and filters start failing. If your pool was built before 2010, a professional equipment assessment is worth the investment.

**Hard water from Collin County municipal supply.** Allen's tap water has elevated calcium hardness. Over time, this causes white scale buildup on tiles, inside heaters, and on salt cells. Regular calcium management prevents cosmetic and mechanical damage.

## Common Pool Problems in Allen, TX

### Spring Pollen Overload

Allen homeowners deal with one of the heaviest pollen seasons in North DFW. Oak pollen creates a thick yellow film on the water surface in March and April. It clogs skimmer baskets within hours, overwhelms filters within days, and introduces nitrogen into the water that feeds algae.

During pollen season, pools in Allen need daily skimming and more frequent filter cleaning — not the standard monthly schedule.

### Equipment Failures in Older Neighborhoods

Pump motors, filter cartridges, and heater elements don't last forever. In Twin Creeks and Montgomery Farm, we regularly see homeowners dealing with pumps that are running louder, using more electricity, and eventually failing without warning.

The smart move is proactive replacement before failure. A pump that's running hot or making grinding noises is telling you it's on borrowed time. Replacing it on your schedule costs less than emergency service on a weekend when every pool company is booked.

### Post-Storm Cleanup

North DFW gets its share of severe storms — heavy rain, wind, and hail. Allen pools take a beating after major weather events. Dirty runoff enters the pool, debris overwhelms the filtration system, and chemical balance gets disrupted within hours.

After any major storm, test your water immediately and clean your filter. Don't wait for the pool to look bad — by then the damage to your chemical balance is already done.

### Freeze Damage

Allen isn't immune to winter freezes. The 2021 and 2022 freeze events damaged thousands of pools across Collin County — cracked pipes, blown pump housings, and broken filter tanks. Pools that had their pumps running through the freeze came through fine. Pools that didn't faced $1,000 to $5,000 in repairs.

If you live in Allen, have a freeze plan. Run your pump continuously when temperatures drop below 32°F. If you have automation, set freeze protection mode. If you don't, set a phone alarm and turn the pump on manually.

## What to Look for in a Pool Service in Allen

Not all pool companies are equal, and Allen homeowners should be selective. Here's what separates reliable service from the companies you'll be switching away from within a year:

**Consistency over promises.** You want the same technician showing up on the same day every week. Reliability is the number one reason homeowners switch pool companies — 65% of Texas pool owners change providers within the first year because of inconsistent service.

**Equipment knowledge.** Allen has a wide variety of pool equipment across different eras of construction. Your service provider should know the difference between a single-speed and variable-speed pump, how to properly clean different filter types, and when a repair makes more sense than a replacement.

**Transparent pricing.** No hidden fees for filter cleaning, shock treatments, or seasonal adjustments. Everything should be included or clearly quoted before the work is done.

**Communication after every visit.** You shouldn't have to wonder whether your pool tech came. A service report with chemical readings, notes on what was done, and any issues spotted should be standard.

**Local presence.** A company based in North DFW that serves Allen specifically is going to understand your water, your weather, and your common pool issues better than a franchise running routes from 30 miles away.

## Seasonal Pool Care Calendar for Allen, TX

**March – April:** Pollen season. Increase skimming to daily. Clean filters every 1 to 2 weeks. Start increasing pump run time as temperatures climb. Test water twice weekly.

**May – June:** Summer ramp-up. Full pump run schedule (8 to 12 hours). Weekly chemical testing and adjustment. Brush walls and steps weekly to prevent algae establishment.

**July – August:** Peak demand. Chlorine consumption is at its highest. Monitor water level for evaporation loss. Shock every 2 to 3 weeks to break down chloramines and organic contaminants.

**September – October:** Transition period. Reduce pump hours slightly. Continue weekly chemical maintenance. Start clearing fall leaf debris as trees begin dropping.

**November – December:** Winter mode. Reduce pump run time to 4 to 6 hours. Set freeze protection. Keep chemicals balanced even though the pool isn't in use — neglected winter chemistry causes spring startup problems.

**January – February:** Freeze risk period. Run pump continuously during any freeze event. Inspect equipment after cold snaps. Begin planning spring startup and any equipment replacements needed.

posts.title: How to Get Your Pool Summer-Ready in Texas (2026 Checklist)
posts.excerpt: Don't start summer behind. Here's the step-by-step checklist to inspect equipment, clean filters, balance chemistry, and avoid costly problems before the heat hits.
posts.content:
Summer in North DFW doesn't ease in — it hits. One week it's 75°F, the next it's 95°F and your pool goes from barely used to daily demand. If your equipment hasn't been inspected, your filter hasn't been cleaned, and your chemistry isn't dialed in before that transition happens, you're starting the season behind.

Most emergency pool service calls in April and May come from homeowners who skipped spring preparation. Pumps that sat idle through winter fail on the first hot weekend. Filters clogged with pollen can't keep up with increased use. Algae that was silently growing in unbalanced water blooms as temperatures rise.

This checklist walks you through everything your pool needs before summer arrives — in order of priority, with the reasoning behind each step.

## Step 1: Inspect All Equipment

Before you worry about water chemistry or cleaning, check that your mechanical systems are healthy. Equipment problems get worse under summer load, not better.

**Pump:** Turn it on and listen. It should run smoothly with no grinding, whining, or rattling. Check for water leaks around the pump housing, fittings, and unions. Look at the pump basket lid — if the O-ring is cracked or flat, replace it now. A $10 O-ring prevents a $1,500 pump replacement.

**Filter:** Check the pressure gauge with the system running. Note the reading — this is your baseline. If it's already high (above 15 to 20 psi for most systems), clean the filter before summer starts. If the gauge doesn't move or reads zero, replace the gauge — they cost $10 to $15 and tell you everything about your filter's health.

**Heater (if applicable):** Turn it on and verify it ignites and heats. Look for error codes on the display. Check for rust, corrosion, or debris around the unit. Gas heaters should have clear venting with no obstructions.

**Automation and timers:** Verify that your pump schedule is set correctly for summer hours. Update the clock if needed (power outages reset some timers). Test all automation features — lights, spa mode, water features.

**Salt cell (if saltwater):** Remove and inspect the cell for calcium buildup. Clean with a diluted acid solution if needed. Check that the generator is producing chlorine properly.

## Step 2: Deep Clean the Filter

Your filter worked all winter trapping dust, pollen, and fine debris. By spring, it's accumulated months of buildup that a quick rinse won't fix.

**Cartridge filters:** Remove the cartridges and soak them in a filter cleaning solution overnight. Rinse thoroughly with a garden hose. Inspect for cracks, tears, or fraying. If your cartridges are over 12 to 18 months old, consider replacing them before summer demand hits.

**Sand filters:** Backwash until the waste water runs clear. If you haven't replaced the sand in 5+ years, now is the time. Old sand channels and loses its ability to trap fine particles.

**DE filters:** Backwash, disassemble the filter tank, and clean each grid individually. Recharge with the correct amount of DE powder. Inspect grids for tears — a torn grid lets DE powder pass into the pool.

A clean filter at the start of summer means better circulation, less chemical demand, and fewer problems all season.

## Step 3: Balance Your Water Chemistry

Spring is the easiest time to balance your water because temperatures are still moderate and chemical reactions are slower. Get everything dialed in now — once summer heat arrives, keeping chemistry balanced becomes a constant battle.

**Test everything:**

- Free chlorine: 1 to 3 ppm
- pH: 7.2 to 7.6
- Total alkalinity: 80 to 120 ppm
- Calcium hardness: 200 to 400 ppm
- Cyanuric acid (stabilizer): 30 to 50 ppm

**Common spring issues in North DFW:**

- pH tends to drift high over winter. Add muriatic acid to bring it back into range.
- Cyanuric acid may have accumulated if you used chlorine tablets all winter. If CYA is above 80 ppm, a partial drain and refill is the only fix.
- Calcium hardness is often elevated from North Texas hard water. Sequestering agents help prevent scale formation.

Take a water sample to a local pool store for a full professional test if you want a complete picture — they'll check for metals, phosphates, and total dissolved solids that your home kit doesn't measure.

## Step 4: Shock the Pool

Even if your water looks clear, give it a strong shock treatment before the season begins. Shocking eliminates any bacteria, algae spores, or organic contaminants that accumulated over the off-season.

Use calcium hypochlorite (cal-hypo) shock at the rate recommended on the package — typically 1 pound per 10,000 gallons for a standard shock, or 2 pounds per 10,000 gallons for a heavy shock.

Run the pump continuously for 24 hours after shocking to ensure full circulation and distribution.

## Step 5: Clean the Pool Thoroughly

With equipment inspected, filter cleaned, chemistry balanced, and the pool shocked, now do a full physical cleaning:

- Skim the entire surface
- Brush all walls, steps, benches, and the waterline tile
- Vacuum the floor — manual vacuum or robotic cleaner
- Empty all skimmer and pump baskets
- Clean the waterline tile with a tile cleaner to remove calcium deposits and body oil residue

This is your clean slate for summer. Everything from here is maintenance, not catch-up.

## Step 6: Check Water Level and Fill System

Winter evaporation and any drain-downs for repairs may have lowered your water level. Fill the pool to the middle of the skimmer opening. Too low and the skimmers suck air, which can damage the pump. Too high and the skimmers can't function properly.

If you have an autofill system, test it now. Make sure the float valve is working correctly and the fill line isn't clogged.

## Step 7: Inspect the Pool Surface and Structure

Walk around the pool and look for:

- Cracks in the plaster or pebble finish
- Loose or missing tiles at the waterline
- Deteriorating coping stones
- Cracks in the deck that could indicate settling
- Damaged or loose drain covers (this is a safety issue)

Address any structural issues before the pool sees heavy summer use. Small cracks become big cracks when water gets in and the Texas heat causes expansion and contraction.

## Step 8: Set Your Summer Maintenance Schedule

Summer in North DFW means your pool works harder than any other time of year. Lock in a weekly routine:

**Daily:** Skim the surface (5 minutes)

**Twice weekly:** Test chlorine and pH, adjust as needed (10 minutes)

**Weekly:** Full brush, vacuum, basket cleaning, chemical check (30 to 45 minutes)

**Monthly:** Deep filter cleaning, full water chemistry panel, equipment inspection

**After every storm:** Test water, clean filter, remove debris, shock if needed

If that sounds like more time than you want to spend, weekly professional service handles all of it. Either way, the schedule matters more than who does it.

## The Cost of Skipping Spring Prep

Here's what deferred maintenance actually costs:

- **Green pool cleanup:** $300 to $1,200
- **Pump replacement (due to overwork from dirty filter):** $800 to $2,000
- **Freeze damage repair (from last winter):** $1,000 to $5,000
- **Filter replacement (from neglected cleaning):** $200 to $600
- **Heater repair (from corrosion/scale buildup):** $300 to $1,500

Total potential cost of skipping prep: **$2,600 to $10,300**

Total cost of proper spring prep: **$200 to $500** if done professionally, or just your time if done yourself.

The math is clear.

---

posts.title: Pool Service in Murphy, TX: What Homeowners Need to Know
posts.excerpt: Murphy pools face heavy leaf load, shaded backyards, and aging equipment. Here's what makes pool maintenance different in this tight-knit Collin County community.
posts.content:
Murphy, TX is one of those North DFW cities that punches above its weight. Just over 20,000 residents, fully built out, and packed with well-maintained homes that almost all have pools. It's a small city where neighbors talk, and when someone finds a good pool company, word spreads fast.

What makes Murphy unique from a pool maintenance perspective is the density. Homes sit on smaller lots compared to Frisco or Prosper, which means pools are closer to fences, landscaping, and neighbor yards. Trees hang over from both sides. Debris comes from everywhere. And because Murphy is fully developed — there's no new construction buffer — every yard has mature landscaping that drops leaves, pollen, and seeds directly into pools.

This guide covers what Murphy homeowners specifically deal with and what to look for in a pool service that understands this tight-knit community.

## Why Murphy Pools Need More Attention Than You'd Think

Murphy may be small, but its pools face the same North DFW challenges as any Collin County city — plus a few of its own.

**Mature tree canopy across the entire city.** Murphy's neighborhoods were largely built between 2000 and 2015. The trees planted during construction are now fully grown. Large oaks, pecans, and Bradford pears hang over fences and drop leaves, pollen, small branches, and seeds into pools constantly. During fall, leaf load in Murphy pools is some of the heaviest in the area.

**Smaller lots mean more landscape debris.** Unlike Prosper or Celina where homes sit on half-acre lots with distance between the pool and the nearest tree line, Murphy backyards are compact. Your neighbor's tree is your pool's problem. There's simply less buffer between landscaping and water.

**Hard water from the city's municipal supply.** Like the rest of Collin County, Murphy's tap water runs high in calcium. This causes scale buildup on tiles, inside heaters, and on salt cells. Without proactive calcium management, the white deposits become harder and more expensive to remove over time.

**HOA communities with pool maintenance expectations.** Many Murphy neighborhoods — Maxwell Creek, Mustang Park, Murphy Heights — have active HOAs that expect well-maintained properties. A green or neglected pool draws attention quickly in a community this close-knit.

## Common Pool Issues in Murphy, TX

### Heavy Leaf and Debris Load

This is the number one issue Murphy pool owners face. The mature trees are beautiful but relentless. During peak leaf season (October through December), skimmer baskets fill up within hours. Leaves that sink to the bottom release tannins that turn water brown if they're not removed quickly.

Professional service during fall typically involves more frequent basket cleaning and additional vacuuming time compared to cities with newer, less mature landscaping.

### Algae Establishment in Shaded Pools

Many Murphy pools sit in partially shaded backyards due to the tree canopy. While shade keeps the water cooler (which swimmers appreciate), it also creates conditions where algae can establish on walls and floors without the UV exposure that normally helps keep surfaces clean.

Shaded pools in Murphy need more aggressive brushing schedules. Algae grows on surfaces long before it becomes visible in the water. By the time the water looks green, the walls are already coated.

### Filter Clogging From Fine Debris

Pollen, dust, and decomposing leaf matter create fine particles that clog filters faster than large debris. Murphy pools typically need filter cleaning every 4 to 6 weeks during spring and fall — more frequently than the standard 8 to 12 week interval in cities with less tree coverage.

Homeowners who skip filter maintenance during heavy debris seasons often end up with cloudy water that won't clear despite proper chemical levels. The filter simply can't keep up.

### Aging Equipment in Original-Build Homes

Most Murphy homes are 10 to 25 years old, which means the original pool equipment is reaching end-of-life. Single-speed pumps that were standard when these pools were built are now outdated, inefficient, and approaching failure.

If your pump is more than 10 years old and your electricity bill seems high, a variable-speed pump upgrade pays for itself within 1 to 2 years through energy savings alone.

## What to Look for in a Pool Service in Murphy

Murphy is small enough that reputation matters more than marketing. Here's what the best pool service providers deliver:

**Adjusted service for heavy debris seasons.** A company that treats every week the same regardless of season isn't paying attention. October in Murphy requires different service than July. Your provider should adapt without you asking.

**Knowledge of older equipment.** Murphy pools often have equipment that's no longer manufactured or has been discontinued. Your technician should know when a repair makes sense versus when it's time to upgrade — and give you an honest answer either way.

**Reliable weekly schedule.** In a city this small, your neighbors know when the pool guy doesn't show up. Consistency matters — same day, same technician, every week.

**Communication.** Service reports after every visit, heads-up when something needs attention, and responsive texting or calling when you have a question. You shouldn't have to wonder what's happening with your pool.

## Seasonal Care Calendar for Murphy, TX

**January – February:** Winter mode. Run pump 4 to 6 hours daily. Run continuously during freeze events. Inspect equipment after cold snaps. Monitor chemistry monthly.

**March – April:** Pollen season. Daily skimming recommended. Clean filter every 2 to 3 weeks. Ramp up pump run time as temperatures climb. Begin shock treatments.

**May – June:** Summer transition. Full pump schedule — 8 to 12 hours. Weekly chemical testing. Brush walls thoroughly to prevent algae in shaded areas.

**July – August:** Peak summer. Maximum chlorine demand. Watch for evaporation — Murphy pools lose water fast in 100°F+ heat. Shock every 2 to 3 weeks.

**September – October:** Fall transition. Leaf drop begins. Increase skimming and basket cleaning frequency. Keep chemicals balanced as temperatures cool.

**November – December:** Heavy leaf season. Filter cleaning every 3 to 4 weeks. Remove leaves from bottom promptly to prevent tannin staining. Prepare freeze protection plan.

## Murphy Neighborhoods Hydra Pool Services Serves

Hydra Pool Services provides weekly maintenance, chemical balancing, equipment repair, and seasonal care throughout Murphy, TX, including:

- Maxwell Creek
- Mustang Park
- Murphy Heights
- Murphy Crossing
- Southgate
- Country Lane Estates
- Bluffs of Murphy
- North Murphy

We also serve neighboring Allen, Plano, Frisco, McKinney, and The Colony.

---

posts.title: How Often Should You Shock Your Pool in Texas?
posts.excerpt: Every 2 weeks in summer, monthly in winter, and immediately after storms. Here's the complete Texas-specific guide to pool shocking — timing, dosage, types, and mistakes to avoid.
posts.content:
Pool shocking is one of those maintenance tasks that most Texas homeowners either do too much, too little, or not at all. Some shock every week because the pool store told them to. Others haven't shocked in months because the water looks fine. Both approaches cause problems.

Shocking your pool means adding a concentrated dose of chlorine (or a non-chlorine oxidizer) to destroy contaminants that regular chlorine levels can't handle — things like bacteria, algae spores, body oils, sunscreen, sweat, and organic debris that build up over time.

In Texas, where pools run nearly year-round and summer temperatures push water above 90°F, the rules are different than what you'll find in generic pool guides written for the Northeast. Here's when and how to shock your pool based on what actually happens in North DFW.

## Why Texas Pools Need Shocking More Than Pools in Other States

Three factors make Texas pools more demanding:

**Heat accelerates everything.** Chemical reactions roughly double in speed for every 18°F increase in water temperature. When your pool hits 90°F in July, chlorine gets consumed faster, bacteria multiply quicker, and organic breakdown accelerates. Regular chlorine dosing can't always keep up.

**UV destroys chlorine.** Texas sun is intense. Ultraviolet radiation can wipe out up to 90% of free chlorine in a two-hour window if cyanuric acid (stabilizer) levels aren't adequate. Even with proper stabilizer, daily chlorine loss is higher here than in less sunny regions.

**Heavy usage patterns.** North DFW homeowners use their pools from March through October — and many swim year-round. More swimmers mean more contaminants. Every person introduces roughly a pint of sweat, sunscreen, body oils, and other organics per hour of swimming. That load adds up.

These factors combine to produce higher levels of chloramines — the "used-up" chlorine molecules that cause the harsh chlorine smell, eye irritation, and skin dryness. Shocking breaks apart chloramines and restores your free chlorine to effective levels.

## The Standard Shock Schedule for North DFW

Here's a practical shock schedule based on Texas conditions:

### Summer (May through September)
**Every 2 weeks** — minimum. During peak heat months, biweekly shocking keeps chloramines in check and prevents algae from getting a foothold.

**Weekly** if: your pool gets heavy daily use, you've had multiple pool parties, or you notice the water smelling like chlorine (that smell is chloramines, not clean chlorine — it means you need MORE shock, not less).

### Spring and Fall (March–April, October–November)
**Every 3 to 4 weeks.** Temperatures are moderate, usage is lower, and chemical demand is reduced. Monthly shocking maintains water quality without overdoing it.

### Winter (December through February)
**Once a month** — if the pool is staying open and the pump is running. Many North DFW homeowners keep their pools operational through winter, and monthly shocking prevents algae from silently establishing during the cooler months.

**Skip shocking** only if you've properly winterized and closed the pool with a winter cover. Even then, check chemistry monthly.

## When to Shock Outside the Regular Schedule

Beyond the routine, certain events should trigger an immediate shock:

**After a rainstorm.** Heavy rain dilutes chlorine, drops pH, and introduces contaminants from runoff. North DFW storms can dump inches of rain in minutes. Shock within 24 hours of any significant storm.

**After a pool party or heavy use.** A single pool party with 8 to 10 swimmers introduces enough organic load to overwhelm your regular chlorine level. Shock the evening of the event or the following morning.

**When you notice a chlorine smell.** This is counterintuitive but critical — a strong chlorine odor means your pool needs shocking, not that it has too much chlorine. That smell is chloramines, and they need to be oxidized out.

**When water looks slightly dull or hazy.** This is early-stage contamination. Shocking now prevents cloudiness from becoming a full algae bloom that costs 10 times more to fix.

**After a freeze event.** If your pool was stagnant during a freeze (pump wasn't running), the lack of circulation may have allowed bacterial growth. Shock when temperatures recover and circulation resumes.

**When you open the pool for the season.** Always start the season with a heavy shock to kill anything that grew during the off-season.

## Types of Pool Shock and Which to Use

Not all shock is the same. Here's what's available and when to use each:

### Calcium Hypochlorite (Cal-Hypo)
The most common and effective. Available at every pool supply store. Contains 65 to 73% available chlorine. Use this for routine shocking and algae treatment.

**Note:** Cal-hypo adds calcium to your water. In North DFW where calcium hardness is already elevated from hard tap water, frequent use can push calcium levels too high. Monitor calcium hardness quarterly and switch to dichlor occasionally if levels are climbing.

**Dosage:** 1 pound per 10,000 gallons for routine shock. 2 pounds per 10,000 gallons for heavy contamination or visible algae.

### Sodium Dichlor (Dichlor)
Granular, dissolves quickly, and contains stabilizer (CYA). Good for pools that need both a chlorine boost and a stabilizer bump. Don't overuse — it adds CYA with every application, and once CYA gets too high (above 80 ppm), chlorine effectiveness drops.

### Non-Chlorine Shock (Potassium Monopersulfate)
Oxidizes contaminants without adding chlorine. Good for breaking down chloramines when your chlorine level is already adequate. You can swim within 15 minutes of application (unlike chlorine shock, which requires waiting until free chlorine drops below 5 ppm).

Use non-chlorine shock when you want to oxidize after a party without spiking chlorine levels.

### Liquid Chlorine (Sodium Hypochlorite)
Same thing as bleach, just stronger concentration (10 to 12.5% vs household bleach at 6 to 8%). Adds no calcium and no CYA — just pure chlorine. Many professional pool technicians in North DFW prefer liquid chlorine for this reason.

**Downsides:** Shorter shelf life, bulky to store, and must be handled carefully.

## How to Shock Your Pool Properly

Shocking incorrectly wastes chemicals and can damage your pool. Follow this process:

**1. Test your water first.** Know your current free chlorine, pH, and CYA levels before adding anything. If pH is above 7.6, bring it down first — chlorine is significantly less effective at high pH.

**2. Calculate your dosage.** Know your pool's volume in gallons. Most residential pools in North DFW are 12,000 to 20,000 gallons. Use the dosage recommended on the product label based on your volume.

**3. Shock in the evening.** UV destroys chlorine. If you shock at 2pm on a sunny day, half your treatment gets burned off before it can do its job. Shock after sunset for maximum effectiveness.

**4. Run the pump for at least 8 hours after shocking.** Circulation distributes the shock treatment throughout the entire pool. Without circulation, you'll have pockets of heavily treated water and pockets of untreated water.

**5. Don't swim until free chlorine drops below 5 ppm.** This typically takes 8 to 24 hours depending on dosage and conditions. Test before allowing swimmers back in.

**6. Brush the pool before or after shocking.** Brushing disrupts any algae film on surfaces and exposes it to the shock treatment. This makes shocking dramatically more effective.

## Common Shocking Mistakes

**Shocking during the day.** UV eats chlorine. You're literally burning money. Always shock after sunset.

**Not running the pump afterward.** If the pump isn't running, the shock just sits in one area of the pool. Full circulation for 8+ hours is non-negotiable.

**Using only non-chlorine shock.** Non-chlorine oxidizers are useful but they don't kill algae. If you have an algae problem, you need chlorine-based shock.

**Ignoring pH before shocking.** At pH 7.2, chlorine is about 65% effective. At pH 8.0, it's only about 20% effective. Shocking a pool with high pH is like washing dishes with cold water — technically you're doing something, but it's not working well.

**Adding shock through the skimmer.** Concentrated chlorine running through your pump and filter can damage equipment. Pre-dissolve granular shock in a bucket of water and pour it around the pool's perimeter, or broadcast it directly into the deep end.

## The Bottom Line

In Texas, shocking is not optional — it's part of the routine. The heat, UV, and usage patterns in North DFW demand more from your pool's sanitization system than regular chlorine alone can deliver.

Stick to the schedule: every 2 weeks in summer, monthly in winter, and immediately after storms, parties, or any sign of water quality decline. Get the timing right (evening), the dosage right (based on your pool volume), and keep the pump running afterward.

If you're unsure whether your pool needs shocking or want professional chemical management, a weekly service that includes regular shock treatments takes the guesswork out entirely.

posts.title: Variable Speed Pool Pumps: Are They Worth the Upgrade?
posts.excerpt: A variable speed pump can save $600/year on electricity. Here's the real math — costs, payback period, and whether the upgrade makes sense for your Texas pool.
posts.content:
Your pool pump is the single biggest electricity consumer in your backyard — and in Texas, where pumps run 8 to 12 hours a day for most of the year, that cost adds up fast. If you're still running a single-speed pump from 2010, you could be paying three to four times more in electricity than you need to.

Variable speed pumps have been around for over a decade, but adoption in North DFW has accelerated in the last few years as energy costs have climbed and older single-speed units have started failing. The question isn't really whether they're better — the engineering is settled on that. The question is whether the investment makes sense for your specific pool, your usage, and your budget.

Here's the straight answer with real numbers.

## How Pool Pumps Work and Why Speed Matters

A pool pump moves water through your filter, heater, and chemical system. It needs to circulate your pool's entire volume at least once per day — that's called a "turnover." For a typical 15,000-gallon North DFW residential pool, one turnover takes roughly 8 hours at standard flow rates.

### Single-Speed Pumps

Single-speed pumps run at one speed — full power, all the time. When the pump is on, it's consuming maximum electricity whether the pool needs that much flow or not. Most single-speed pumps in residential pools draw **1,500 to 2,500 watts** continuously.

This is like driving everywhere in 5th gear at full RPM. It works, but it's wildly inefficient.

### Variable Speed Pumps

Variable speed pumps use a permanent magnet motor (similar to an electric car motor) that can run at any speed from low to high. For routine daily filtration, they run on low speed — typically **200 to 500 watts** — and only ramp up to full speed when needed for tasks like vacuuming, running a waterfall, or operating a spa.

Since most of your pump's daily work is just moving water through the filter, low speed handles 90% of the job at a fraction of the electricity cost.

## The Physics That Makes This Work

This is where it gets interesting. There's a principle in fluid dynamics called the Affinity Law that governs pump energy consumption. In simple terms: **when you cut pump speed in half, you cut energy consumption by roughly 87%.**

That's not a typo. Running a pump at half speed doesn't use half the electricity — it uses one-eighth. The relationship between speed and energy is cubic, not linear.

So a pump running at 1,500 RPM instead of 3,450 RPM uses dramatically less power while still moving enough water to filter your pool effectively. It just takes longer to complete a turnover — which is fine, because you have 24 hours in a day and the pump can run on low speed for 12 to 16 hours while barely moving the electricity meter.

## Real Cost Comparison for North DFW

Let's use actual Texas electricity rates and typical pool sizes.

**Assumptions:**
- Pool size: 15,000 gallons
- Electricity rate: $0.12 per kWh (average for Collin County residential)
- Pump runs 10 hours per day average (weighted across seasons)

### Single-Speed Pump

- Power draw: 2,000 watts (2.0 kW)
- Daily cost: 2.0 kW × 10 hours × $0.12 = **$2.40 per day**
- Monthly cost: **$72**
- Annual cost: **$864**

### Variable Speed Pump (running mostly on low)

- Average power draw: 500 watts (0.5 kW) with occasional high-speed bursts
- Daily cost: 0.5 kW × 12 hours × $0.12 = **$0.72 per day**
- Monthly cost: **$22**
- Annual cost: **$264**

### Annual Savings: $600

Over a typical pump lifespan of 8 to 12 years, that's **$4,800 to $7,200** in electricity savings from a single equipment change.

## What Does a Variable Speed Pump Cost?

**Equipment cost:** $800 to $1,800 depending on brand, horsepower, and features. Premium brands like Pentair IntelliFlo, Hayward Super Pump VS, and Jandy VS FloPro are all reliable options commonly installed in North DFW.

**Installation cost:** $200 to $500 for a straightforward replacement where the plumbing and electrical connections are compatible. Occasionally an older installation needs electrical upgrades or plumbing modifications, which can add to the cost.

**Total installed cost:** $1,000 to $2,300

**Payback period:** At $600 per year in savings, most homeowners recoup the investment in **18 to 30 months**. After that, the savings are pure profit on your electricity bill every single month.

## Beyond Energy Savings — Other Benefits

### Quieter Operation

Single-speed pumps are loud — 65 to 75 decibels, roughly the volume of a vacuum cleaner running for 10 hours a day. Variable speed pumps on low speed run at 40 to 50 decibels — barely noticeable from inside the house.

In Murphy and Allen where houses sit close together, this is a real quality-of-life upgrade for you and your neighbors.

### Better Filtration

This is counterintuitive, but running a pump on low speed for longer hours actually produces better filtration than running it on high speed for fewer hours. Water moves through the filter more slowly, giving the filter media more time to trap fine particles. The result is clearer water with less chemical demand.

### Longer Equipment Lifespan

Lower speed means less mechanical stress on the pump, less wear on bearings and seals, and less strain on plumbing connections. Variable speed pumps typically last 8 to 12 years versus 5 to 8 years for single-speed units. Less vibration also means fewer plumbing leaks over time.

### Reduced Chemical Usage

Better circulation and filtration means chemicals distribute more evenly throughout the pool. Dead zones where algae typically starts are eliminated. Many North DFW homeowners report 10 to 20% reduction in chemical costs after switching to variable speed.

## When Does a Variable Speed Pump NOT Make Sense?

There are a few situations where the upgrade isn't the best use of money:

**Your pool has other major problems.** If your plaster is failing, your filter is shot, or you have active leaks, fix those first. A new pump on a broken system is like putting premium gas in a car with a blown engine.

**You're selling the house within a year.** You won't recoup the investment before the sale — though a variable speed pump does add value to the property listing.

**Your current pump is less than 3 years old and working fine.** Unless your electricity bill is genuinely painful, let the current pump run its life before upgrading.

**Your pool is very small (under 8,000 gallons).** The savings scale with pool size. Very small pools already have low pump costs, so the payback period stretches longer.

For everyone else — especially homeowners with pools built before 2015 that still have original single-speed pumps — the variable speed upgrade is one of the best returns on investment in pool ownership.

## Texas Regulations Worth Knowing

As of 2021, the Department of Energy mandated that all new pool pumps sold in the United States must meet specific energy efficiency standards. In practice, this means most new replacement pumps available today are either variable speed or dual speed — single-speed pumps above 1 HP are being phased out.

If your current pump fails, you'll likely be replacing it with a variable speed unit anyway. The question is whether you do it proactively (while choosing your timing and avoiding emergency pricing) or reactively (when it fails on a Saturday in July and you're paying emergency rates).

## What Hydra Pool Services Recommends

We install variable speed pumps across Frisco, Plano, McKinney, Allen, Murphy, and The Colony. We've seen the real-world impact on our customers' electricity bills and water quality — it's significant.

If your pump is over 8 years old, making noise, or your electricity bill seems high for a residential pool, a variable speed upgrade is worth evaluating. We can assess your current setup, recommend the right size and model for your pool, and give you an honest quote with no pressure.

posts.title: Pool Service in The Colony, TX: What Homeowners Need to Know
posts.excerpt: Living near Lewisville Lake means higher humidity, more algae pressure, and unique wind patterns. Here's what Colony pool owners need to know about year-round maintenance.
posts.content:
The Colony sits right on Lewisville Lake in Denton County — and that location shapes everything about pool ownership here. The lake proximity means higher humidity, more mosquitoes, and different wind patterns than inland cities like Frisco or McKinney. It also means more homeowners with pools, because if you live near water, you tend to want water in your backyard too.

The Colony has grown steadily over the last decade, with newer communities like Tribute and Castle Hills bringing modern pool builds alongside the established neighborhoods near Stewart Creek Park and Five Star Complex that have pools from the early 2000s. This mix of new and old creates different maintenance needs across the city.

Here's what The Colony pool owners deal with and how to keep your pool in top shape year-round.

## What Makes The Colony Different for Pool Owners

### Lake Proximity and Humidity

Living near Lewisville Lake means higher ambient humidity compared to cities further east like Allen or Murphy. Higher humidity affects pools in subtle but real ways:

**Slower evaporation** — your pool won't lose water as fast as pools in drier areas, which means less frequent refilling but also less natural dilution of concentrated chemicals.

**Increased algae pressure** — humid air carries more organic particles and algae spores. Pools in The Colony tend to develop algae faster than pools just 10 miles inland if chlorine levels drop even briefly.

**More mosquito activity around the pool** — standing water in skimmer baskets, unused pool covers, and equipment pad areas attract mosquitoes. Regular maintenance reduces breeding sites.

### Wind Patterns From the Lake

The Colony gets lake breeze — wind that comes off Lewisville Lake carrying fine sand, organic matter, and moisture. This wind pushes more airborne debris into pools than you'd experience in a landlocked neighborhood.

Pools on the west side of The Colony (closer to the lake) see noticeably more surface debris than pools on the east side. If your pool faces the prevailing wind, a windscreen or strategic landscaping can reduce the load on your skimmers and filter.

### New Construction vs Established Neighborhoods

**Tribute and Castle Hills** — newer communities with modern pool equipment. Many have automation systems, variable speed pumps, and saltwater setups. These pools need less repair work but require technicians who understand modern systems.

**Established neighborhoods near Stewart Creek and Camino Real** — pools here are 15 to 20+ years old. Equipment is aging, plaster may be wearing thin, and original single-speed pumps are approaching or past end of life. These pools need more repair attention and proactive equipment replacement planning.

**HOA communities** — several Colony neighborhoods have HOA pools or require homeowners to maintain their private pools to community standards. Consistent service prevents the complaints and violations that come with a neglected pool.

## Common Pool Problems in The Colony

### Algae After Rain Events

The Colony gets hit by the same North DFW storms as everyone else, but the lake proximity adds an extra factor — runoff. Heavy rain events wash organic material and phosphates into pools more aggressively in lower-lying areas near the lake. Phosphates are algae food, and elevated phosphate levels after storms are a common trigger for green pools in The Colony.

After any major storm, test your water and shock within 24 hours. If you've had recurring post-storm algae problems, ask your service provider about phosphate remover treatments as a preventive measure.

### Scale Buildup From Hard Water

The Colony draws its water from the same North Texas supply as the rest of Denton County — and it's hard. Calcium hardness out of the tap is elevated, which means ongoing scale management is necessary.

Scale shows up as white deposits on waterline tiles, inside heaters, on salt cells, and around return jets. Left untreated, scale reduces equipment efficiency and looks terrible. Regular acid washing of tiles and descaling of equipment prevents the buildup from becoming a major project.

### Equipment Aging in Older Neighborhoods

Pools built between 2000 and 2010 are now in the window where major equipment components start failing. Pumps, heaters, and filter tanks all have finite lifespans. The Colony homeowners in older neighborhoods should be planning for equipment replacements rather than waiting for emergency failures.

Proactive replacement lets you choose timing, compare prices, and schedule installation during off-peak months when pool companies have availability. Emergency replacement in the middle of July costs more and takes longer.

## Seasonal Pool Care for The Colony

**January – February:** Winter maintenance. Run pump 4 to 6 hours daily. Continuous operation during freeze events. Monthly chemistry checks. Inspect equipment for freeze damage after cold snaps.

**March – April:** Spring startup. Pollen management — daily skimming, filter cleaning every 2 to 3 weeks. Ramp up pump run time. Full chemistry rebalance. Consider a spring shock treatment.

**May – June:** Summer transition. Full pump schedule. Weekly chemical management. Algae prevention becomes the priority — lake humidity increases algae pressure.

**July – August:** Peak demand. Maximum chlorine consumption. Monitor water level for evaporation. Shock biweekly. Clean filter monthly minimum.

**September – October:** Transition. Leaf drop begins. Reduce pump hours slightly. Maintain chemistry even as usage drops.

**November – December:** Winter prep. Inspect and service equipment before cold weather. Set freeze protection if you have automation. Keep chemicals balanced through the off-season.

## The Colony Neighborhoods Hydra Pool Services Serves

Hydra Pool Services provides weekly maintenance, chemical balancing, filter cleaning, and equipment repair throughout The Colony, TX, including:

- Tribute
- Castle Hills
- The Shores
- Camino Real
- Stewart Peninsula
- Stewart Creek Estates
- Austin Waters
- Legends of The Colony

We also serve neighboring Frisco, Plano, McKinney, Allen, and Murphy.

posts.title: How to Fix a Green Pool Fast (Texas Step-by-Step Guide)
posts.excerpt: Pool turned green? Here's the exact 10-step process to go from swamp to swim-ready — with dosages, timing, and the mistakes that make it take twice as long.
posts.content:
You walked outside and your pool is green. Maybe it happened overnight after a storm, maybe it crept in over a week while you were busy. Either way, you're standing in front of a swamp and you want it fixed fast.

Green pool water means algae has taken over. In North DFW, where water temperatures climb above 80°F from May through September and UV burns through chlorine in hours, algae blooms happen fast — sometimes in as little as 24 to 48 hours if chlorine drops to zero.

The good news: a green pool is fixable. The bad news: there's a right way and a wrong way to do it. Most homeowners waste money dumping chemicals in randomly. This guide gives you the exact process — step by step, in order — to go from green to clear as fast as possible.

## Why Your Pool Turned Green

Before you start treating, understand what happened. Algae doesn't appear randomly — it grows when conditions are right:

**Chlorine dropped to zero or near zero.** This is the most common cause. You skipped a few days of chemical maintenance, your chlorinator ran out, or a heavy rainstorm diluted your chlorine. Without sanitizer, algae spores that are always present in the air land in the water and start multiplying immediately.

**Filter wasn't running enough.** Poor circulation creates dead zones — areas where water sits still. Algae establishes in these spots first, then spreads. If your pump was off for a day or two, or running too few hours, that's likely a contributing factor.

**After a storm.** Rain introduces phosphates, nitrogen, and organic debris that feed algae while simultaneously diluting your chlorine. Post-storm algae blooms are extremely common in North DFW from March through October.

**CYA (stabilizer) is too high.** If cyanuric acid levels exceed 80 ppm, chlorine becomes ineffective even at normal levels. The chlorine is there on paper, but it can't do its job. This is a sneaky cause that many pool owners miss.

**You were on vacation.** A week without maintenance in Texas summer is enough for a pristine pool to turn fully green. It's one of the most common calls pool companies get in June and July.

## Assess the Severity

Not all green pools are equal. The treatment approach depends on how bad it is.

**Light green / teal tint — you can still see the bottom:** Early-stage bloom. Fastest to fix. Usually 24 to 48 hours to clear with proper treatment.

**Dark green — can't see the bottom but can see a few inches down:** Moderate bloom. Expect 2 to 4 days of treatment with aggressive shocking and continuous filtration.

**Black-green — can't see anything, looks like a pond:** Severe bloom. This takes 4 to 7 days minimum. The filter will need cleaning multiple times during the process. Consider whether professional service saves you time and money at this stage.

## Step 1: Test Your Water

Before adding anything, test these levels:

- **Free chlorine** — likely near zero if the pool is green
- **pH** — needs to be between 7.2 and 7.4 for shock treatment to work effectively
- **CYA (stabilizer)** — if this is above 80 ppm, your shock treatment will be significantly less effective and you may need a partial drain

**Critical:** If pH is above 7.6, bring it down with muriatic acid BEFORE shocking. Chlorine is three times more effective at pH 7.2 than at pH 7.8. Shocking a high-pH pool wastes chemicals and extends the recovery time.

## Step 2: Clean Out the Big Stuff

Before you add chemicals, remove as much physical debris as possible.

- Use a leaf net (not a fine-mesh skimmer) to scoop out leaves, sticks, and large clumps of algae
- Empty all skimmer baskets
- Clean the pump strainer basket
- Do NOT vacuum yet — you'll just clog the filter immediately

You're not trying to make the pool look good at this stage. You're removing organic matter that would consume your shock treatment before it can kill algae.

## Step 3: Clean or Backwash Your Filter

Your filter is about to do the hardest work of its life over the next few days. It needs to start clean.

**Cartridge filter:** Remove cartridges, spray them thoroughly with a hose, and reinstall. If they're more than a year old and the pool is severely green, consider replacing them — old cartridges can't handle the particle load from an algae cleanup.

**Sand filter:** Backwash for 3 to 5 minutes until waste water runs clear.

**DE filter:** Backwash, disassemble if possible, clean grids, and recharge with fresh DE powder.

You will need to clean the filter again — possibly multiple times — during the recovery process. As dead algae gets filtered out, it clogs the filter fast. Plan on cleaning it every 12 to 24 hours during heavy treatment.

## Step 4: Brush Everything

This step is critical and most homeowners skip it. Algae clings to pool surfaces — walls, floor, steps, behind ladders, inside crevices. If you don't brush it off the surfaces, the shock treatment can't reach it effectively.

Brush the entire pool aggressively. Walls, floor, steps, benches, waterline tile, around drains, behind ladders. The water will look worse afterward — that's fine. You're breaking the algae loose so the chemicals and filter can do their job.

Use a steel-bristle brush for plaster/concrete pools. Use a nylon brush for fiberglass or vinyl.

## Step 5: Shock the Pool — Hard

This is not a maintenance shock. This is a kill dose.

**For light green (can see the bottom):**
2 pounds of calcium hypochlorite per 10,000 gallons.

**For dark green (can't see the bottom):**
3 to 4 pounds of calcium hypochlorite per 10,000 gallons.

**For black-green (pond status):**
4 to 5 pounds of calcium hypochlorite per 10,000 gallons. At this severity, you may need to repeat in 24 hours.

**Pre-dissolve granular shock in a bucket of water** before adding to the pool. Broadcast around the perimeter, focusing on areas where algae is thickest. Do NOT add shock through the skimmer — concentrated chlorine damages pump seals and filter internals.

**Shock in the evening.** UV destroys chlorine. Shocking at 2pm in Texas sun wastes 40 to 50% of your treatment before it can work.

## Step 6: Run the Pump Continuously

After shocking, run the pump 24 hours a day until the pool clears. No exceptions. The filter needs to process every gallon of water multiple times to remove dead algae particles.

Check filter pressure every 8 to 12 hours. When pressure rises 8 to 10 psi above clean baseline, stop and clean the filter again. Then restart. You may need to do this 2 to 4 times during a severe cleanup.

## Step 7: Add Algaecide (After Shocking, Not Before)

Wait 24 hours after shocking, then add a quality algaecide — preferably a copper-based or polyquat formula. Don't use cheap algaecide from the big box store — it foams, stains, and barely works.

Algaecide prevents surviving spores from re-establishing while the filter works to clear the dead algae from the water. It's insurance, not the primary treatment.

## Step 8: Monitor and Repeat

Over the next 2 to 5 days:

- **Test chlorine every 12 hours.** If free chlorine drops below 5 ppm, add more shock. You need to maintain a high chlorine level until the algae is completely dead.
- **Clean the filter every 12 to 24 hours** as needed based on pressure readings.
- **Brush the pool daily** to dislodge any remaining algae from surfaces.

The water will go from green → cloudy green → cloudy white → hazy → clear. Each stage means progress.

## Step 9: Vacuum Dead Algae

Once the water is cloudy white (no green tint), the algae is dead but the particles are still suspended. At this point:

- If you have a multiport valve, vacuum to WASTE — this sends the dead algae out of the pool entirely instead of through the filter. You'll lose water, so run a hose to refill simultaneously.
- If you can't vacuum to waste, vacuum normally but clean the filter immediately afterward.

## Step 10: Final Balance and Prevention

Once the water is clear:

- Test and balance all chemistry — chlorine, pH, alkalinity, calcium hardness, CYA
- Clean the filter one final time
- Resume normal pump schedule
- Consider adding phosphate remover to eliminate algae food sources

Most importantly — figure out why the pool went green and fix the root cause. If it was neglected maintenance, commit to a weekly routine. If it was a storm, build post-storm treatment into your habits. If it was high CYA, address that with a partial drain.

## What This Costs

**DIY green pool cleanup:**
- Shock: $30 to $80 depending on severity
- Algaecide: $15 to $30
- Filter cleaning supplies or replacement cartridges: $0 to $200
- Muriatic acid and other chemicals: $15 to $30
- Your time: 8 to 20 hours over 3 to 7 days
- **Total: $60 to $340 plus significant time investment**

**Professional green pool cleanup:**
- Light green: $200 to $400
- Moderate green: $400 to $800
- Severe (pond): $800 to $1,500
- **Includes all chemicals, multiple visits, filter cleaning, and guaranteed results**

For moderate to severe green pools, professional cleanup often makes more financial sense when you factor in the value of your time and the risk of doing it wrong (which extends the process and costs more in chemicals).

## How to Make Sure It Never Happens Again

- Maintain chlorine between 1 and 3 ppm at all times — test twice weekly in summer
- Run your pump 8 to 12 hours daily during warm months
- Shock every 2 weeks in summer, monthly in winter
- Clean your filter on schedule — don't wait until the water tells you
- Shock within 24 hours of any major rainstorm
- Monitor CYA quarterly — keep it below 60 ppm
- If you travel, arrange for someone to maintain the pool or hire a service

A green pool is preventable. Consistent weekly maintenance costs a fraction of a single green pool cleanup — and it keeps your pool ready to swim every day.

---

posts.title: Is Your Pool Losing Water? How to Tell If It's a Leak
posts.excerpt: Dropping water level — evaporation or a leak? Here's how to tell the difference, where leaks hide, what they cost to ignore, and when to call a pro.
posts.content:
Your pool's water level dropped again. You filled it two days ago and it's already low. Maybe it's evaporation — Texas heat does that. Or maybe your pool is leaking, and every day you wait is water wasted, chemicals diluted, and potential structural damage getting worse underground where you can't see it.

Pool leaks are one of the most expensive problems to ignore. A small leak — even a pinhole in a pipe — can waste 10,000 to 20,000 gallons of water per month. That's $100 to $200 in water bills alone, plus the constant chemical rebalancing, plus the risk of undermining your deck, foundation, or yard.

The tricky part is that not every water loss is a leak. Texas evaporation is aggressive. Splash-out from swimming adds up. Backwashing your filter uses water. Knowing the difference between normal water loss and an actual leak saves you from either ignoring a real problem or panicking over nothing.

## Normal Water Loss vs. a Leak

### What's Normal in North DFW

During peak summer (June through August), evaporation can account for a quarter inch to half inch of water loss per day in an uncovered pool. Over a week, that's 1.5 to 3.5 inches — which is significant and completely normal.

Factors that increase evaporation:
- High temperatures (above 95°F)
- Low humidity
- Wind exposure
- The pool is unshaded
- Water features running (waterfalls, bubblers, fountains increase surface area)

If your pool has a water feature that runs continuously, it can increase evaporation by 50% or more compared to a still pool.

### What's NOT Normal

- Losing more than half an inch per day consistently
- Water level dropping when the pool isn't being used and no backwashing was done
- Wet spots in the yard that shouldn't be there
- Cracks appearing in the deck or coping
- Air bubbles consistently coming from return jets
- The autofill system running constantly
- Needing to add water more than twice a week in summer or at all in winter

If any of these describe your situation, it's time to investigate.

## The Bucket Test: Confirm Whether You Have a Leak

This is the standard test every pool professional uses as a starting point. It isolates evaporation from actual water loss.

**How to do it:**

1. Fill a 5-gallon bucket with pool water and set it on the first or second step of your pool, submerged so the water inside the bucket is level with the pool water outside.
2. Mark the water level inside the bucket AND the pool water level outside the bucket with tape or a marker.
3. Wait 24 hours. Don't swim, don't run water features, don't backwash. Keep the pump running on its normal schedule.
4. After 24 hours, compare the two levels.

**Reading the results:**

- If the pool level dropped MORE than the bucket level, you likely have a leak. Both should lose the same amount to evaporation — the difference is your leak.
- If both dropped the same amount, you're losing water to evaporation only. No leak.
- If the pool dropped more than a quarter inch beyond the bucket, the leak is significant enough to investigate.

**Run the test twice** for confirmation — once with the pump running and once with the pump off. If the water loss is worse with the pump on, the leak is likely in the plumbing (pressure side). If it's worse with the pump off, the leak may be in the pool shell or suction side.

## Common Leak Locations in North DFW Pools

### Underground Plumbing

This is the most common and most expensive leak source. PVC pipes running underground from the equipment to the pool can develop cracks from soil movement, tree root pressure, or expansion and contraction from Texas temperature swings.

North DFW has expansive clay soil that swells when wet and contracts when dry. This constant movement stresses underground pipes over time. Pools built on clay soil in Frisco, McKinney, and Allen are particularly susceptible.

Signs of a plumbing leak:
- Soggy or unusually green patches of grass between the pool and equipment pad
- Erosion or sinkholes near the pool
- Air in the system (bubbles from return jets)
- Loss of pump prime

### Equipment Pad

Leaks at the equipment pad are often visible if you look carefully. Check around:
- Pump housing and lid
- Filter tank and multiport valve
- Heater connections
- Pipe unions and fittings
- Chlorinator or salt cell connections

Even a slow drip from a fitting can waste hundreds of gallons per month. Tightening a union or replacing an O-ring often fixes equipment pad leaks for under $20.

### Pool Shell

Cracks in plaster, pebble, or fiberglass surfaces can allow water to seep through. These are less common than plumbing leaks but do occur, especially in older pools or pools that have experienced ground movement.

Look for:
- Visible cracks in the plaster (not just surface crazing, which is cosmetic)
- Cracks near fittings, lights, or skimmer mouths
- Tiles that have popped off the waterline
- Separation between the pool shell and the coping

### Light Fixtures and Fittings

The area around pool lights, return jets, skimmer mouths, and the main drain are common leak points. The sealant around these penetrations degrades over time, creating paths for water to escape.

A conduit leak behind a pool light is one of the most common leaks pool professionals find in North DFW. It's often invisible from inside the pool but shows up as water loss that stops when the pool level drops below the light niche.

### Skimmer Body

The joint where the skimmer body meets the pool wall is a stress point. Ground movement, settling, or age can cause this joint to separate slightly. If you notice the water level consistently stops dropping right at the bottom of the skimmer mouth, the leak is likely at the skimmer.

## What a Leak Costs If You Ignore It

**Water waste:** A 1-inch-per-day leak in a standard pool loses approximately 500 gallons daily. That's 15,000 gallons per month — roughly $150 to $200 in water bills depending on your municipality's rates.

**Chemical waste:** Every time you add water, you dilute your chemicals and need to rebalance. Over a month, this adds $50 to $100 in unnecessary chemical costs.

**Structural damage:** Water escaping underground can erode soil beneath your deck, causing settling, cracking, and even sinkholes. Deck repair costs $2,000 to $10,000+ depending on severity.

**Equipment damage:** A leak on the suction side introduces air into the system, which causes the pump to run dry periodically. Dry running destroys pump seals and motors — a $800 to $2,000 replacement.

**Foundation risk:** In severe cases, persistent underground water loss near a home's foundation can contribute to foundation movement. This is rare but the most expensive possible outcome.

**Total cost of ignoring a moderate leak for one year:** $3,000 to $15,000+

**Cost of professional leak detection:** $250 to $500

**Cost of most leak repairs:** $300 to $1,500

The math always favors finding and fixing leaks early.

## When to Call a Professional

DIY leak detection has limits. The bucket test tells you IF you have a leak. Finding WHERE the leak is requires specialized equipment:

- **Pressure testing** — pressurizes each plumbing line individually to identify which pipe is leaking
- **Electronic listening devices** — detect the sound of water escaping underground
- **Dye testing** — uses colored dye near suspected leak points to visualize water flow
- **Camera inspection** — sends a camera through plumbing lines to find cracks

If your bucket test confirms a leak, call a leak detection specialist rather than starting to dig up your yard. A professional can pinpoint the exact location and often fix the issue the same day, minimizing disruption and cost.

## Preventing Leaks

Not all leaks are preventable, but you can reduce your risk:

- **Maintain proper water chemistry.** Acidic water (low pH) corrodes pipes and fittings from the inside. Keep pH between 7.2 and 7.6.
- **Run the pump during freezes.** Frozen water expands and cracks pipes. This is the single most preventable cause of pool leaks in North DFW.
- **Address soil movement.** If you notice your deck shifting or cracking, investigate before the movement affects underground plumbing.
- **Replace old O-rings and gaskets.** Equipment pad fittings use rubber seals that dry out and crack over time. Replace them during annual equipment inspections.
- **Don't ignore small signs.** A slightly soggy spot in the yard, a pump that occasionally loses prime, or a water bill that's crept up — these are early warnings. Acting on them early saves thousands.

---

We'll find it, fix it, and save you from the damage that comes with waiting. [Schedule an inspection →](https://hydrapoolservices.com/contact)
